# Advancing Policy Initiatives for Equitable Access

Women leaders can work with educational institutions and governmental bodies to advance policies that ensure equitable access to technology and tech education. By advocating for initiatives such as providing affordable devices and internet access to underserved communities, they can play a crucial role in democratizing tech education.

Women leaders can work with educational institutions and governmental bodies to advance policies that ensure equitable access to technology and tech education. By advocating for initiatives such as providing affordable devices and internet access to underserved communities, they can play a crucial role in democratizing tech education.

# Leveraging Digital Technologies to Advance Women’s Economic Empowerment

![Photo: Sean Gallup/Getty Images](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/styles/500_x_300/s3/2024-08/GettyImages-635626602_Crop1.jpg?VersionId=azsd3aVfj44qP1bXsVZPOY7pdZGuQZLZ&h=266da715&itok=g4B9uCBh)

Photo: Sean Gallup/Getty Images

## Table of Contents

## Table of Contents

[Report](/taxonomy/term/3013)
by 

[Romina Bandura](/people/romina-bandura) 
and 
[Madeleine McLean](/people/madeleine-mclean)

Published August 27, 2024

### Available Downloads

#### **Introduction**

Despite worldwide advances in digitization over the past decade, many people are still excluded from the digital economy and miss out on the opportunities it provides. This exclusion, referred to as the [digital divide](https://downloads.bbc.co.uk/mediaaction/pdf/india-research-study-women%E2%80%99s-digital-literacy-2021.pdf)—or “the gap between those who can access the dividends and opportunities of the digital economy, and those who cannot”—is particularly pronounced for women. The challenges women face in accessing the digital ecosystem include obtaining formal identification (ID), owning mobile phones, and possessing digital skills, among others. The gender digital divide has serious repercussions at the household level and in broader society. By 2025, developing economies are anticipated to be missing out on [$1.5 trillion](https://www.unwomen.org/sites/default/files/2022-09/Progress-on-the-sustainable-development-goals-the-gender-snapshot-2022-en_0.pdf) in GDP because women do not have full access to the digital economy.

While the gender digital divide persists, there is considerable opportunity for change. Through the private sector and government institutions, the United States can contribute significantly to closing the gender digital divide abroad and enabling digital public infrastructure (DPI) for women. Actions the United States can undertake include expanding existing programs, working with partners to enact gender-inclusive laws and regulations, building secure digital ecosystems, and supporting digital literacy initiatives for women.

#### **Gender Gaps in the Digital Ecosystem**

Across the digital ecosystem, women are less likely than men to access affordable physical infrastructure (e.g., [electricity](https://trackingsdg7.esmap.org/data/files/download-documents/sdg7-report2023-ch1._access_to_electricity.pdf), internet, and mobile phones) and digital services (e.g., identification documents, digital payments, and e-commerce and e-government). Women are also [underrepresented](https://professionalprograms.mit.edu/blog/leadership/the-gender-gap-in-stem/) in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) as well as jobs and occupations related to information and communications technology (ICT), such as developing [artificial intelligence](https://www.unwomen.org/en/news-stories/explainer/2024/05/artificial-intelligence-and-gender-equality) (AI) and software applications. Overall, they also possess lower digital literacy than men. For more vulnerable women, such as those living in [rural](https://press.un.org/en/2020/sgsm20343.doc.htm) areas, from Indigenous groups, or living with disabilities, this divide is even worse; the more challenges women face, the larger the digital divide grows.

***Gaps in Accessing Physical Infrastructure***

According to the International Energy Agency, approximately [770 million](https://www.iea.org/topics/energy-and-gender) people globally cannot access reliable electricity. This gap in access to electricity is mainly concentrated in Africa and Asia. Unfortunately, women bear a disproportionate burden in terms of reliable access to electricity, forcing them to use [traditional](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-024-00202-9) energy sources, such as firewood or charcoal, for their households or businesses*.* Not only does this place strain on women’s time as women have to gather and transport these fuels, but they are also large contributors to indoor air pollution, impacting the health of the women using them. Moreover, [785 million](https://www.gsma.com/r/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/The-Mobile-Gender-Gap-Report-2024.pdf?utm_source=website&utm_medium=button&utm_campaign=gender-gap-2024) women in the developing world do not have mobile internet access, and approximately [40 percent](https://www.gsma.com/r/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/The-Mobile-Gender-Gap-Report-2024.pdf?utm_source=website&utm_medium=button&utm_campaign=gender-gap-2024) of women in developing countries do not own a smartphone. The [trade-offs](https://www.csis.org/events/leveraging-digital-technologies-empower-women) and opportunity costs of connecting to the internet or purchasing a device are often much higher for women, who are less likely to receive legal identification at birth and may have reduced control over household resources.

The gender gap in internet usage remains prominent in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). Although women have increased their access to the internet since 2019, there is still significant disparity compared to men (Figure 1). According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), there are [244 million](https://www.itu.int/itu-d/reports/statistics/2023/10/10/ff23-the-gender-digital-divide/) more men than women using the internet in 2023. Africa has the largest gender digital divide: only [32 percent](https://www.itu.int/itu-d/reports/statistics/2023/10/10/ff23-the-gender-digital-divide/) of women have access to the internet in 2023, compared to 42 percent of men.

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig1.png?VersionId=rv6fHd3_GBYH.I2xSaUXZwpcIw9P.h_V)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig1.png?VersionId=rv6fHd3_GBYH.I2xSaUXZwpcIw9P.h_V)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig1.png?VersionId=rv6fHd3_GBYH.I2xSaUXZwpcIw9P.h_V)

In terms of mobile ownership, the same pattern applies to income groups and regions (Figure 2). Regionally, Africa and the Asia-Pacific region have the largest gender gaps in mobile ownership, at 11.2 percent and 8.1 percent, respectively, in 2023

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig2.png?VersionId=6sP1O9oJFcSLz7LdFx332yfHDgQyk8Yw)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig2.png?VersionId=6sP1O9oJFcSLz7LdFx332yfHDgQyk8Yw)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig2.png?VersionId=6sP1O9oJFcSLz7LdFx332yfHDgQyk8Yw)

***Gaps in Digital Services***

Women face similar challenges accessing digital services. According to the [Identification for Development](https://id4d.worldbank.org/global-dataset) (ID4D) data set, approximately 850 million people globally do not have an official ID, and women are a large share of this group (Figure 3). Across Latin America and the Caribbean, South Asia, and sub-Saharan Africa, the gender gap in [ID coverage](https://databank.worldbank.org/source/identification-for-development-(id4d)-data) in 2021 hovered just below 10 percent.

Lack of ID poses a serious barrier for women entering the digital economy, as financial institutions often require a government ID to open and use accounts. When analyzing the data on financial inclusion and digital payments, women also face hurdles in getting credit and making payments, which hampers their ability to finance social and economic activities (Figures 4 and 5). The differences in these indicators for women in low-income countries versus advanced economies are quite stark.

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig3.png?VersionId=tF9EScbI.pORcP0ty7rlU5X51yVkG8RC)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig3.png?VersionId=tF9EScbI.pORcP0ty7rlU5X51yVkG8RC)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig3.png?VersionId=tF9EScbI.pORcP0ty7rlU5X51yVkG8RC)

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig4.png?VersionId=3KSglL6yU7w4r9WAeVGEtwNXYJpnbXNf)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig4.png?VersionId=3KSglL6yU7w4r9WAeVGEtwNXYJpnbXNf)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig4.png?VersionId=3KSglL6yU7w4r9WAeVGEtwNXYJpnbXNf)

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig5.png?VersionId=6xRWkXql0RsWhH3tSGsg1JjeIoxDQ2Kf)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig5.png?VersionId=6xRWkXql0RsWhH3tSGsg1JjeIoxDQ2Kf)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/2024-08/240827_Bandura_Women_fig5.png?VersionId=6xRWkXql0RsWhH3tSGsg1JjeIoxDQ2Kf)

In sum, [women in developing countries](https://blogs.worldbank.org/digital-development/her-future-digital) are more likely than men to lack official government ID, less likely to own smartphones, and more likely to be unbanked or underbanked.1 This marginalizes women from mainstream economic activities and society at large.

#### **Using Digital Services to Enhance Women’s Economic Empowerment**

Providing better access to digital services can be an important enabler for women’s economic empowerment, but it does not necessarily guarantee it. There are other impediments such as [social and patriarchal norms](https://www.gsma.com/r/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/The-Mobile-Gender-Gap-Report-2024.pdf?utm_source=website&utm_medium=button&utm_campaign=gender-gap-2024) that prohibit women from undertaking many economic activities. For example, women may be prohibited from buying a smartphone, using the internet, or using a mobile device in public. These challenges underlie the gender divide, and they generally cannot be solved by introducing a different set of technologies into the ecosystem.

Nevertheless, there are many examples of how developing countries have used digital services to reach marginalized women. During the Covid-19 pandemic, Togo made emergency digital payments to its citizens, paying more women than men, including those in the informal economy. Because Togo had existing digital infrastructure, the country set up these programs quickly and efficiently (in [10 days](https://www.weforum.org/agenda/2021/08/4-reasons-you-should-care-about-digital-public-infrastructure/)), providing much-needed financial support to its citizens. Similarly, the government of India used its Aadhaar identification system to reach over [200 million](https://www.orfonline.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/T20_PB_TF2_219_DigitalFinancialInclusion-1.pdf) women and provide emergency financial support during the Covid-19 pandemic. In addition, India’s [Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana](https://www.pmjdy.gov.in/) program, which ensures easy access to financial services, has been expanded to focus on women, creating the Jan Dhan Plus program. The program encourages low-income women to save regularly; emphasizes the rewards of saving by linking it to access to microcredit, insurance, and pensions; and improves the gender sensitivity of banking agents. As of April 2023, Jan Dhan Plus had engaged over [12 million](https://www.womensworldbanking.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/DPI_Policy_Brief.pdf) female customers, increasing their average savings by over 50 percent in just five months and doubling enrollments in microinsurance and pension schemes.

Beyond financial inclusion, digital services may also be applied to other sectors—for example, in agriculture. Women are responsible for [60–80 percent](https://pdf.usaid.gov/pdf_docs/pnadr706.pdf) of agricultural production in the developing world, yet they routinely lack access to relevant farming information and financial assistance. It is estimated that if women-owned farms had equal access to inputs (such as farm equipment, livestock, diversified seed options, fertilizers, and digital technologies), they would increase sub-Saharan Africa’s agricultural output by [10–20 percent](https://pdf.usaid.gov/pdf_docs/pnadr706.pdf). Through digital tools, women can obtain [information](https://www.csis.org/events/leveraging-digital-technologies-empower-women) such as weather data, policy updates, and communications on their legal rights. With access to weather data, women farmers can better assess when, where, and what to plant. Likewise, if women can access information on laws, regulations, and other policies, they will have more tools to protect and grow their businesses.

#### **Building Digital Tools with Women, for Women**

As noted above, providing women with these technological tools will not automatically spur gender equity and economic activity. Women must be included in the development of the digital ecosystem so that these tools better serve their needs and are relevant to their daily lives. Digital technologies can empower women, but when they are not designed around women’s experiences and needs, they risk “growing different divides and exacerbating different challenges.”2 Excluding women in the design and implementation of DPI has many downsides, including replicating or integrating biases and offering digital products that ultimately are ineffective for women. According to [Tim Wood](https://www.weforum.org/agenda/2024/03/digital-public-infrastructure-blessing-or-curse-for-women-and-girls/), chief partnerships officer of Co-Develop, a nonprofit fund that helps partners adopt safe and inclusive shared DPI at scale, “In the architecture of DPI, gender equity must be more than an afterthought; it must be the blueprint guiding every decision. We have an opportunity now to address the gender inequalities of the physical world in the digital landscape.”

In the architecture of DPI, gender equity must be more than an afterthought; it must be the blueprint guiding every decision. We have an opportunity now to address the gender inequalities of the physical world in the digital landscape.

When women are [excluded](https://www.appen.com/blog/the-power-of-gender-equity-in-building-a-diverse-and-inclusive-world) from designing and developing digital tools, biases may unintentionally be introduced into the system. The analysts that train these language models might overrepresent certain demographic characteristics over others (e.g., white men under 40) and thus inadvertently skew the programming away from women. When programs make decisions based on these AI models—from credit limits to healthcare and employment decisions—women might miss out, even in an advanced country such as the United States. As an example, in 2019, Apple’s [digital credit card](https://www.bbc.com/news/business-50365609) assigned different credit limits to women and men regardless of limited differences in salary, debt, and credit scores. In one case, one man’s line of credit was approximately [20 times larger](https://ssir.org/articles/entry/when_good_algorithms_go_sexist_why_and_how_to_advance_ai_gender_equity) than his wife’s, and customer service employees were unable to explain the AI-generated decision. Another example comes from [Thailand](https://itforchange.net/sites/default/files/2352/ITFC_Gender%20by%20Design%20White%20Paper.pdf), where the national digital ID system has led to some trafficking survivors being excluded from financial institutions because their data include a history involving human trafficking.

In addition, women need to feel confident and safe in the digital world so that they can realize the benefits and mitigate the risks of digitization. This implies that digital infrastructure must be built in such a way that women’s privacy and data are protected. As women gain access to digital services and interact with cyberspace, they need to trust the digital ecosystem. For this reason, the cybersecurity of the system is paramount: women and other vulnerable communities could fall victim to bad actors if DPI systems do not have strong security protocols. A [survey](https://globaldigitalinclusion.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/CoE-Report.pdf) conducted by the Alliance for Affordable Internet (A4AI) showed that women in Colombia, Ghana, and Uganda reported greater fear of data privacy leaks than men.

In addition, women should be better equipped with [digital literacy](https://www.csis.org/analysis/digital-literacy-imperative), ranging from basic skills, such as knowing how to use hardware, software, and the internet, to more advanced skills in ICT professions, such as computer programming and network management. Many jobs related to innovations such as AI, big data, natural-language processing, cybersecurity, the Internet of Things (IoT), software development, and digital entrepreneurship require advanced digital skills. However, only [22 percent](https://www.unesco.org/en/articles/gender-biases-ai-and-emerging-technologies) of AI and data science professionals are women. As discussed, this underrepresentation may affect the type of digital services provided to women.

#### **Expanding Digital Services for Women: The Role of the United States**

Given the opportunities DPI presents for women’s economic empowerment, there is growing momentum globally to strengthen programs in developing countries. The United States has been a champion of digitization and can become a leader and game changer in the DPI space. Working with the private sector, U.S. government agencies such as the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID), the Millennium Challenge Corporation (MCC), and the U.S. Trade and Development Agency (USTDA) can provide technical expertise to build a more gender-inclusive DPI in the developing world. By encouraging the adoption of [secure](https://www.csis.org/analysis/advancing-digital-transformation-and-digital-public-infrastructure-role-private-sector), ethical, and inclusive digital systems, there are a number of ways the United States can leverage its assistance to create women-centered digital ecosystems:

**Support digital literacy initiatives abroad.** Not only do digital platforms need to be secure, but women must also be equipped with adequate digital skills to interact in cyberspace. The United States can play a bigger role in this area. The [private sector](https://www.unesco.org/en/articles/gender-biases-ai-and-emerging-technologies) could be a valuable partner by providing funding and expertise to educational institutions, such as vocational schools and higher education institutions, to provide scholarships and courses for women and girls in ICT. Below are several examples of programs the United States provides abroad:

#### **Conclusion**

As the digital landscape continues to evolve, the United States has a unique opportunity to empower women in this space. By taking concrete steps to address the gender digital divide, equip women entrepreneurs, and foster secure digital environments, the United States can catalyze change on a global scale. However, the full benefits of digitization cannot be achieved without a [coordinated strategy](https://dial.global/gender-inclusion-and-digital-public-infrastructure/) to include women throughout the design and implementation processes. Women must have a seat at the table in the digital transformation journey so that they can shape their economic destinies.

*Romina Bandura is a senior fellow with the Project on Prosperity and Development at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) in Washington, D.C. Madeleine McLean is a program coordinator and research assistant with the Project on Prosperity and Development at CSIS.*

*The authors would like to thank Salome Girgvliani for her excellent research support. This paper also benefited immensely from the insights of a CSIS event held on March 5, 2024.*

# Bridging the Gap: How Can We Ensure Equal Technology Access for Women?

Promoting gender equality in technology involves increasing public awareness, implementing gender-inclusive policies, creating educational and women-centric tech spaces, supporting female tech entrepreneurship, fostering inclusive company cultures, leveraging technology to bridge access gaps, engaging men as allies, improving resource and infrastructure access, and monitoring progress for accountability.

Promoting gender equality in technology involves increasing public awareness, implementing gender-inclusive policies, creating educational and women-centric tech spaces, supporting female tech entrepreneurship, fostering inclusive company cultures, leveraging technology to bridge access gaps, engaging men as allies, improving resource and infrastructure access, and monitoring progress for accountability.

![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/unhelpgul-whitebg.svg)
![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/helpful-whitebg.svg)
![](/sites/default/files/default_images/Women%20in%20Tech%20profile%20picture.png)

### Increasing Public Awareness and Advocacy

To ensure equal technology access for women, a concerted effort must be made to increase public awareness about the disparities in technology access. Advocacy campaigns can highlight existing gaps, thus mobilizing communities, governments, and organizations to take action. These campaigns can also promote the benefits of gender equality in technology for society as a whole.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Implementing Gender-Inclusive Policies

Governments and companies can play a significant role by implementing policies that actively promote gender inclusivity. This includes setting quotas for women's representation in tech fields, offering incentives for companies that achieve gender parity, and enforcing laws that prevent gender discrimination in technology access and employment.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Creating Educational Programs

To bridge the gap, educational systems should incorporate technology-focused curriculum that is accessible and appealing to girls from an early age. This includes creating STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) programs specifically designed to engage girls, providing scholarships for women in tech, and implementing mentorship programs that connect young women with female leaders in technology.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Developing Women-Centric Technology Spaces

Establishing women-centric spaces, such as technology hubs, workshops, and online communities, can provide a supportive environment for women to explore, learn, and innovate in technology. These spaces can also serve as platforms for networking, mentorship, and sharing resources and knowledge.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Encouraging Female Entrepreneurship in Tech

Supporting and promoting female entrepreneurship in technology fields can significantly contribute to closing the gender gap. This can be achieved through funding, training, and mentorship programs tailored for women-led tech startups, alongside creating networks that connect women entrepreneurs with investors who are committed to gender equality.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Fostering a Culture of Inclusion and Diversity within Tech Companies

Tech companies should actively work towards creating a culture of inclusion and diversity. This involves not only recruiting more women but also ensuring they have equal opportunities for career advancement, leadership roles, and participating in decision-making processes. Providing flexible work hours and fair parental leave policies can also make the tech industry more appealing to women.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Utilizing Technology to Bridge the Gap

Ironically, technology itself can be a powerful tool in ensuring equal access for women. Online courses, webinars, and digital resources can provide learning opportunities for women who might not have access to traditional education due to geographical, financial, or social constraints. Blockchain and AI can also be utilized to create transparent systems for hiring and promotion that eliminate bias.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Engaging Men as Allies

Achieving equal technology access for women isn't solely a women's issue; it requires the active participation of men. Programs and workshops that engage men and boys as allies in promoting gender equality in technology can lead to more inclusive environments. Encouraging men to support their female colleagues, challenge discriminatory practices, and advocate for equal opportunities is essential.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Providing Access to Resources and Infrastructure

In many parts of the world, women's access to technology is limited by a lack of basic resources and infrastructure, such as electricity, internet connectivity, and digital devices. Initiatives aimed at improving infrastructure, along with programs that provide women with technology tools and resources, are crucial steps toward bridging the technology gap.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Monitoring Progress and Accountability

To ensure that efforts to bridge the technology gap are effective, it is essential to monitor progress and hold institutions accountable. This can involve collecting and analyzing gender-disaggregated data, setting measurable goals for gender equality in technology access and representation, and publicly reporting on progress. Establishing mechanisms for accountability ensures that commitments translate into action and tangible outcomes.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### What else to take into account

This section is for sharing any additional examples, stories, or insights that do not fit into previous sections. Is there anything else you'd like to add?

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Advocate for policies ensuring affordable devices and internet access for marginalized groups, thereby democratizing technology education and bridging the gender digital divide.
Encourage programs that provide mentorship, funding, and training tailored for women in technology, fostering female entrepreneurship and enhancing representation in STEM fields.

enriched_points.point_title: Encourage Diverse Representation in Data Governance
enriched_points.expanded_content: Ensure women and marginalized communities are included in data governance discussions, promoting ethical standards and diverse perspectives in technology decision-making processes.
